narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score and plans to attend full-time, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection from our law school admissions process. Our model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 58.9% chance that you would fail it on your first try, which tipped the scales against your application since we prioritize those likely to pass and succeed in practice. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, well below the typical student's 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model that your later academic performance might not hold up under law school pressures. This outweighed your strong LSAT score of 38, which is above the average of about 37 and pushed the prediction toward success more than any other positive factor. Your Year 1 decile of 3rd, also lower than the usual 6th decile, added some additional concern about your early grades, though less so than your later ones. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 fell short of the typical 3.2, nudging things slightly toward the risk of failure, while your lower-middle family income bracket and full-time status offered minor positives compared to averages. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students who boast stronger decile rankings across the board, making the model view you as higher-risk despite your LSAT edge. We're rooting for you, and to strengthen future applications, consider retaking courses to boost your deciles, aiming for a higher UGPA through post-bac programs, or gaining legal experience to demonstrate resilience. You have the LSAT foundation—building on your academic trajectory could turn this around. Keep pushing forward; many successful lawyers faced early setbacks like this. If you'd like feedback on specific steps, our admissions team is here to guide you.
